Web Mining · Sentiment Analysis · Natural Language Processing · 2026

Monitoring Public Mood Ahead of the Toy Story 5 Premiere: A Web Mining Analysis of Sentiment and Emotion in 16,510 YouTube Trailer Comments

Submitted June 2026  ·  YouTube Data API v3  ·  VADER & DistilRoBERTa  ·  Google Colab T4
Md. Shimul Ahmed Bappi Undergraduate Aspiring Researcher Department of Statistics and Data Science
Jahangirnagar University, Savar, Dhaka 1342, Bangladesh
shimulbappi.sds49@gmail.com GitHub LinkedIn ResearchGate
16,510Comments Analysed
45.8%Positive Sentiment
0.061Weighted Score
4 mo.Observation Window
7Emotion Categories
Abstract

The release of a major cinematic production generates substantial public discourse on digital platforms, with YouTube comment sections serving as a real-time barometer of mass audience sentiment. This study presents a systematic web mining analysis of 16,510 public comments harvested from the official Toy Story 5 (2026) trailer on YouTube, covering the period 19 February–18 June 2026. Sentiment classification was performed using the VADER lexicon-based model; emotion detection used the fine-tuned DistilRoBERTa-base model (j-hartmann/emotion-english-distilroberta-base) on a GPU-accelerated Google Colab T4. Results show 45.8% positive, 35.5% neutral, and 18.8% negative sentiment. The dominant emotion was neutral (50.3%), followed by joy (17.3%) and surprise (15.3%). Like-weighted sentiment yielded a score of 0.061 (mild positive approval). Temporal analysis confirmed a consistently positive mood trajectory throughout the observation window. These findings demonstrate the analytical value of YouTube comment mining for real-time public opinion surveillance in entertainment media contexts.

Index Terms: YouTube mining sentiment analysis emotion detection VADER DistilRoBERTa Toy Story 5 NLP web mining audience reception social media analytics
Table of Contents
  1. Introduction
  2. Background and Related Work
  3. Methodology
  4. Results
  5. Discussion
  6. Conclusion
  7. References
I Introduction

The convergence of social media platforms and participatory digital culture has transformed the way audiences engage with entertainment media. YouTube, the world's largest video-sharing platform, hosts official trailers for major theatrical releases and simultaneously functions as a public forum in which millions of viewers express opinions, emotions, and expectations in the comment section. These unstructured, user-generated text data represent a valuable yet underutilised corpus for studying pre-release audience sentiment at scale.

The Toy Story franchise, produced by Pixar Animation Studios and distributed by Walt Disney Pictures, occupies a singular position in popular culture. Having commenced in 1995 with the first computer-generated feature film of its kind [1], the franchise has accumulated over USD 3.1 billion in global box office revenue across four instalments [2]. The announcement and trailer release of Toy Story 5 (2026) generated immediate and widespread public reaction. Monitoring and quantifying this reaction prior to the film's theatrical premiere constitutes a research problem of both scholarly and commercial significance.

Computational sentiment analysis and opinion mining from online user-generated content has evolved into a mature sub-discipline of natural language processing (NLP). Lexicon-based models such as VADER [3] remain state-of-the-art for short, informal text. More recent transformer-based models such as BERT [4] and its distilled derivatives have demonstrated superior performance on fine-grained emotion classification tasks [5]. Combining both paradigms enables a richer characterisation of audience mood than either approach alone.

This study's specific objectives are: (i) to collect 16,510 YouTube comments via the YouTube Data API v3; (ii) to classify sentiment using VADER; (iii) to detect fine-grained emotions using a GPU-accelerated DistilRoBERTa model; (iv) to identify predominant discussion topics through NLP keyword frequency analysis; (v) to track temporal sentiment evolution via rolling-average analysis; and (vi) to apply like-weighted sentiment scoring to produce a spam-adjusted audience approval metric.

II Background and Related Work

A. Sentiment Analysis in Social Media

Sentiment analysis, or opinion mining, refers to the computational identification and categorisation of subjective information in source materials [6]. Applied to social media, it enables researchers to assess public opinion at a scale unattainable through traditional survey methodologies. The VADER model, proposed by Hutto and Gilbert [3], was specifically engineered for social media text and incorporates grammatical heuristics including capitalisation, punctuation, and degree modifiers to produce compound scores from −1 to +1. Its rule-based architecture confers interpretability without requiring domain-specific training data, making it suitable for informal comment text [10].

B. Transformer-Based Emotion Classification

The introduction of BERT by Devlin et al. [4] marked a watershed in NLP. Distilled architectures, including DistilRoBERTa [11], reduce parameter count while retaining approximately 97% of upstream task performance. Hartmann et al. [5] published the emotion-english-distilroberta-base model, fine-tuned for multi-class emotion classification across the Ekman [12] taxonomy: anger, disgust, fear, joy, neutral, sadness, and surprise — the seven categories employed in this study.

C. YouTube Comment Mining for Cultural Analytics

YouTube comment sections have been recognised as productive data sources for cultural analytics. Thelwall [14] identified engagement asymmetries showing that high-like comments tend to carry more positive polarity — a mechanism directly exploited in this study's like-weighted metric. Siersdorfer et al. [15] examined comment voting as a quality signal. In entertainment analytics, NLP has been applied to movie trailer comments to predict box office performance [16] and to track franchise sentiment [17].

III Methodology

A. Data Collection

The target data source was the official Toy Story 5 trailer (YouTube video ID: c51ND9Hdbw0). Comment data were harvested using the YouTube Data API v3 via the Google API Python Client library. An exhaustive pagination procedure was implemented: the commentThreads.list endpoint was called iteratively with maxResults=100 per request until nextPageToken returned null. Fields extracted: comment text (plain text), publication timestamp, like count, and author display name. The total corpus comprised 16,510 top-level comments spanning 19 February–18 June 2026.

B. Sentiment Analysis (VADER)

The VADER SentimentIntensityAnalyzer [3] was used to compute a compound score per comment. Comments with compound ≥ 0.05 were labelled Positive, those ≤ −0.05 Negative, and the remainder Neutral — in accordance with VADER's standard thresholds.

C. Emotion Detection (DistilRoBERTa)

Fine-grained emotion classification used j-hartmann/emotion-english-distilroberta-base [5] via the Hugging Face Transformers library, deployed on an NVIDIA T4 GPU in Google Colab. Comments were processed in batches of 64 with truncation enabled for inputs exceeding 512 tokens.

D. Topic Modelling via NLP Keyword Frequency

Each comment was pre-processed by: (i) lowercasing; (ii) removing URLs and HTML via regular expressions; (iii) removing punctuation; (iv) tokenising via NLTK word_tokenize; and (v) filtering against an augmented stopword list (standard English stopwords + domain terms: toy, story, movie, film, like, get, one, would, even, really, good, bad). The 15 highest-frequency tokens were retained.

E. Temporal Sentiment Analysis

Daily mean VADER compound scores were computed by grouping comments by publication date. A 3-day rolling average (min_periods=1) was applied to smooth day-level noise, visualised with a y=0 neutral reference line.

F. Like-Weighted Sentiment Scoring

A like-weighted sentiment score was computed as Σ(SentimentScore × LikeCount) / Σ(LikeCount), assigning greater analytical weight to viewer-endorsed comments and producing a spam-adjusted audience approval metric.

G. Cross-Tabulated Emotion–Sentiment Heatmap

A cross-tabulation was computed with sentiment polarity as the row variable and emotion label as the column variable, normalised within each sentiment group to row percentages, and visualised as an annotated heatmap using seaborn.

IV Results

A. Dataset Overview

The exhaustive pagination procedure yielded 16,510 top-level comments spanning 19 February–18 June 2026 — a four-month window capturing the complete pre-premiere public response to the trailer.

B. Sentiment Distribution

7,561Positive45.8% of corpus
5,853Neutral35.5% of corpus
3,096Negative18.8% of corpus

VADER sentiment classification revealed a predominantly positive audience reception. Positive comments (7,561; 45.8%) form the largest group, followed by Neutral (5,853; 35.5%) and Negative (3,096; 18.8%). The negative proportion is less than half the neutral count, indicating a clearly favourable pre-premiere mood.

Fig. 1. Sentiment Distribution across 16,510 YouTube comments from the Toy Story 5 official trailer, classified via VADER. Positive: 7,561 (45.8%); Neutral: 5,853 (35.5%); Negative: 3,096 (18.8%).

C. Emotion Distribution

DistilRoBERTa identified neutral as the most frequent emotion (8,308; 50.3%), followed by joy (2,858; 17.3%), surprise (2,524; 15.3%), sadness (1,036; 6.3%), disgust (731; 4.4%), anger (644; 3.9%), and fear (409; 2.5%). The high neutral proportion reflects the volume of matter-of-fact or exclamatory comments that lack strong discrete emotion signals despite positive VADER scores.

Fig. 2. Emotion Distribution across 16,510 comments classified by DistilRoBERTa. Neutral dominates at 50.3%, followed by joy (17.3%) and surprise (15.3%).

D. Cross-Analysis: Emotion by Sentiment Group

The cross-tabulated heatmap (Fig. 3) reveals distinct emotional profiles per sentiment group. Within the Positive group, neutral (42.8%) and joy (29.3%) dominate with notable surprise (15.2%). The Negative group features neutral (41.2%), sadness (16.1%), and disgust (12.2%), confirming that critical comments carry heavier negative affect. The Neutral sentiment group is overwhelmingly the neutral emotion label (64.8%), validating internal consistency between the two models.

Fig. 3. Emotion vs. Sentiment cross-tabulation heatmap (row-normalised, % of sentiment group). Positive comments feature joy (29.3%) and neutral (42.8%); Negative comments exhibit sadness (16.1%) and disgust (12.2%).

E. Top Discussion Topics

NLP keyword frequency analysis identified the top 15 thematic tokens (Fig. 4). The most discussed term was "woody" (~1,660), followed by "toys" (~1,350), "looks" (~1,060), "see" (~900), and "back" (~850). Character names (Woody, Buzz, Pixar) and thematic terms (back, kids, wait, friend, old) dominated, confirming franchise nostalgia and character anticipation as the central discussion axes. The appearance of "iPad" in the word cloud reflects audience commentary on the new tablet-toy character revealed in the trailer.

Fig. 4. Top 15 cleaned discussion topics by frequency (after custom stopword filtering). "Woody" (1,660), "toys" (1,350), and "looks" (1,060) were the most frequently discussed terms.
Fig. 5. Discussion Word Cloud — full-corpus lexical landscape (before stopword filtering). Dominant terms: Woody, Story, Bonnie, Pixar, kid, back, movie, iPad, wait, friend.

F. Temporal Sentiment Trend

The 3-day rolling average of daily mean VADER scores (Fig. 6) reveals that sentiment remained consistently above the neutral baseline (y=0) throughout the 19 February–18 June 2026 observation window. A notable peak occurred around 20 May 2026 (reaching ~0.28). A single brief dip below zero occurred near 12 May 2026, likely corresponding to a specific news event or social media controversy. The overall positive trajectory confirms sustained audience enthusiasm across four months of pre-premiere discourse.

Fig. 6. Public Mood Trend over time (3-day rolling average of VADER compound scores, Feb–Jun 2026). Sentiment remained above the neutral baseline (dashed red line) for nearly the entire observation window, peaking at ~0.28 in late May 2026.

G. Like-Weighted Sentiment

Like-weighted analysis produced an aggregate score of 0.061, indicating mild positive audience approval. The like-distribution by sentiment shows Positive comments captured 42.7% of total likes, Negative comments 35.3%, and Neutral comments 22.0% (Fig. 7). Notably, Negative comments captured 35.3% of likes despite comprising only 18.8% of comments — nearly double their proportional share — suggesting that articulate critical voices attracted disproportionate viewer endorsement.

Fig. 7. Like-Weighted Sentiment distribution. Positive comments attract 42.7% of total likes; Negative 35.3% (disproportionately high); Neutral 22.0%. Weighted sentiment score: 0.061 (mild positive approval).
V Discussion

The results converge on a consistent narrative of broadly positive pre-premiere public mood for Toy Story 5, while also revealing important structural nuances in audience affect. The 45.8% positive sentiment against 18.8% negative establishes a clear positive majority. However, the neutral dominance in the emotion distribution (50.3%) warrants interpretation: a large share of comments were factual, exclamatory, or descriptive without strong discrete emotional loading — typical of reaction-type comments (e.g., "I can't believe this is happening") that VADER may score positively but the emotion classifier correctly identifies as contextually neutral.

The most illuminating finding concerns the like-weighted analysis. Despite Negative comments comprising only 18.8% of the corpus, they captured 35.3% of total likes — nearly double their proportional share. This suggests that critical or dissenting voices attracted disproportionate viewer endorsement, consistent with Thelwall's [14] observations of polarised engagement dynamics on YouTube. Articulate critiques of franchise continuation after Toy Story 4's thematic closure [17] likely account for this pattern, corroborated by topic keywords "end" and "back."

The top discussion topics validate the emotional landscape: "woody," "back," "kids," "old," "wait," and "friend" collectively paint a picture of an audience animated by nostalgia and franchise loyalty. The presence of "iPad" reflects a distinct subplot of discussion concerning the new tablet-toy character — a generational contrast with the original cast that divided viewer opinion.

Temporally, the single dip below the neutral baseline (~12 May 2026) followed by the highest recorded peak (~0.28, ~20 May 2026) suggests an external catalyst briefly suppressed sentiment before a rapid audience rebound. Identifying such inflection points has direct utility for entertainment marketers monitoring campaign health in real time.

Limitations: (i) Only top-level comments were collected; reply threads were excluded. (ii) VADER may misclassify sarcasm and domain-specific idioms. (iii) DistilRoBERTa truncates inputs beyond 512 tokens. (iv) Both models are optimised for English; multilingual comments may have been misclassified. (v) The study is bounded to the pre-premiere period; post-release sentiment comparison remains future work.

VI Conclusion

This study demonstrates that YouTube comment sections constitute a viable and analytically rich data source for monitoring pre-premiere public mood around major cinematic releases. By applying an integrated web mining pipeline to 16,510 comments from the official Toy Story 5 trailer (February–June 2026), this research establishes that: (i) pre-premiere sentiment was predominantly positive (45.8%); (ii) neutral and joy were the dominant emotional responses; (iii) the sentiment trend remained consistently positive across four months; (iv) like-weighted scoring yielded a mild positive approval score of 0.061; and (v) character nostalgia (Woody, Buzz) and franchise legacy were the central themes of audience discourse.

The methodological framework — combining VADER, DistilRoBERTa, NLP topic extraction, temporal rolling-average analysis, and like-weighted sentiment scoring — offers a replicable template for computational audience analysis applicable to any YouTube-hosted media event. Future work should extend this framework to multilingual corpora, incorporate reply-thread analysis, develop predictive models for box office performance, and conduct post-release sentiment comparison to assess forecast accuracy.

Acknowledgements. The author thanks the Department of Statistics and Data Science at Jahangirnagar University for the academic environment that supports undergraduate research. Gratitude is extended to the open-source communities behind NLTK, Hugging Face Transformers, VADER, and the Google API Python Client. Computational resources were provided by Google Colaboratory.
